point_001: The benchmark is restricted to English-language text classification tasks only, limiting its applicability to multilingual settings and non-classification NLP tasks such as generation or structured prediction.
point_002: Insufficient detail is provided in the main paper about the prompt template design and task-to-QA conversion process for the UniFew baseline, making replication difficult.
point_003: The list of tasks is relegated to the appendix rather than being presented in the main paper, despite being central to understanding the benchmark.
point_004: The benchmark does not account for variance across different prompt formulations and label descriptions; given known sensitivity to prompt choice, reporting results across multiple prompts would be more rigorous.
point_005: The selection of datasets appears ad hoc and driven by low-level criteria (e.g., class balance) rather than higher-level motivations such as practical few-shot scenarios, diverse reasoning types, or tests for spurious correlations.
point_006: The benchmark aggregates existing datasets without introducing new tasks or evaluation methodologies, raising questions about the novelty and added value beyond repackaging prior work.
point_007: The large number of combined settings (transfer type, number of shots, meta-learning strategy) makes the benchmark complex to navigate, report on, and interpret in practice.
point_008: The choices of number of shots and number of classes are not sufficiently justified empirically; more systematic experiments supporting these design decisions would strengthen the work.
point_009: Entity typing is underrepresented—only one dataset (CoNLL) is included—and it is not evaluated under class transfer or domain transfer settings, limiting coverage of this task type.
point_010: The paper does not discuss whether existing benchmarks like GLUE or SuperGLUE could be adapted into few-shot benchmarks, which would allow direct comparison between few-shot and fully supervised performance on the same tasks.
point_011: The limitations section does not address potential biases in the few-shot support examples used for fine-tuning, which could systematically affect model behavior and fairness.
point_012: The proposed UniFew baseline is closely related to concurrent work (T0/Sanh et al., arXiv 2104.04670) that is not cited or discussed, and this relationship should be acknowledged.
